original_problem: A snail is at the bottom of a 20-foot well. Each day, it climbs up 3 feet, but at night, it slips back 2 feet. On the day the snail reaches the top of the well, it climbs out and doesn't slip back. There is a frog at the 15-foot mark that has been watching the snail. The frog decides to join the snail on its journey, but only if the snail reaches the 15-foot mark on a Wednesday. If the snail started climbing on a Sunday, will the frog join the snail, and how many days will it take for the snail to climb out of the well?
deconstructed_problem: A snail is at the bottom of a 20-foot well. Each day, it climbs up 3 feet, but at night, it slips back 2 feet. On the day the snail reaches the top of the well, it climbs out and doesn't slip back. There is a frog at the 15-foot mark that has been watching the snail. The frog decides to join the snail on its journey, but only ଯଦି (if) the snail reaches the 15-foot mark on a Wednesday. ଯଦି (if) the snail started climbing on a Sunday, will the frog join the snail, and how many days will it take for the snail to climb out of the well?
original_steps: Step 1: Determine the daily progress of the snail by calculating the difference between the distance it climbs during the day and the distance it slips back at night.

Step 2: Calculate how many days it takes for the snail to reach the 15-foot mark by dividing the distance to the 15-foot mark by the daily progress, considering that the progress may vary on the final day when it doesn't slip back.
Step 3: Establish the pattern of days of the week to figure out on which day the snail reaches the 15-foot mark, knowing it started climbing on a Sunday.
Step 4: Decide whether the frog will join the snail based on the day of the week the snail reaches the 15-foot mark.
Step 5: Calculate the total number of days it takes for the snail to climb out of the well, considering its daily progress and the final climb without slipping back.
skeleton_steps: ପଦକ୍ଷେପ (step) 1: Determine the daily progress of the snail by calculating the ପାର୍ଥକ୍ୟ (difference) between the distance it climbs during the day and the distance it slips back at night.

ପଦକ୍ଷେପ (step) 2: Calculate how many days it takes for the snail to reach the 15-foot mark by dividing the distance to the 15-foot mark by the daily progress, considering that the progress may vary on the final day when it doesn't slip back.
ପଦକ୍ଷେପ (step) 3: Establish the pattern of days of the week to figure out on which day the snail reaches the 15-foot mark, knowing it started climbing on a Sunday.
ପଦକ୍ଷେପ (step) 4: Decide whether the frog will join the snail based on the day of the week the snail reaches the 15-foot mark.
ପଦକ୍ଷେପ (step) 5: Calculate the ମୋଟ (total) number of days it takes for the snail to climb out of the well, considering its daily progress and the final climb without slipping back.
final_answer: The frog will join the snail, and it will take the snail 18 days to climb out of the well.
